---
title: "Rahul Manavalan"
description: "Academic portfolio in scientific computing, dynamical systems, and numerical analysis."
listing: false
---

::: {.hero}
::: {.hero-text}
# Rahul Manavalan

### Scientific Computing · Dynamical Systems · Numerical Analysis

I work on variational formulations, continuous-time flows, and structure-preserving numerical algorithms for scientific computing.

[Research](research.qmd){.btn .btn-primary} [Publications](publications.qmd){.btn .btn-outline} [Blog](blog.qmd){.btn .btn-outline}
:::

::: {.hero-card}
**Current theme**

Variational problem  
$\rightarrow$ flow/operator  
$\rightarrow$ discretization  
$\rightarrow$ algorithm

**Keywords**

gradient flows · momentum flows · PDEs · Krylov methods · kernels · UQ
:::
:::

## Selected research directions

::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### Flow-based solvers

Exponential and inertial flows for linear systems, inverse problems, and constrained optimization.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### Kernel and spline methods

Boundary-aware interpolation, generalized observations, and approximation on structured domains.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### Imaging and PDEs

Dissipative and inertial PDE models for restoration, inpainting, and inverse problems.
:::
:::

## Recent notes

```{=html}
<div class="callout-note">
  <strong>Edit this template:</strong> replace the placeholder email, CV, publication metadata, and project descriptions with your real details.
</div>
```

## Latest blog posts

```{=html}
<div class="post-grid">
  <a class="post-card" href="posts/gradient-flows-linear-solvers/">
    <span class="post-tag">Numerical analysis</span>
    <h3>Gradient Flows for Linear Solvers</h3>
    <p>A short note on viewing classical iterative methods as discretized flows.</p>
  </a>
  <a class="post-card" href="posts/nonlinear-rkhs-regression/">
    <span class="post-tag">Dynamical systems</span>
    <h3>Nonlinear RKHS Regression as a Flow</h3>
    <p>Sketching a dissipative PDE viewpoint for nonlinear regression.</p>
  </a>
</div>
```
